_meta.authoringNotes: Cross-verified against AAO BCSC Section 7 (Oculofacial Plastic and Orbital Surgery 2022-2023), JAMA Ophthalmology orbital fracture reviews, Soparkar et al. 1994 silent sinus syndrome series, and Ellis-Manor orbital reconstruction outcomes literature.

h1Title: Enophthalmos: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Enophthalmos is posterior displacement of the eyeball within the bony orbit, producing a visibly sunken eye and a deepened upper lid sulcus. The single most frequent cause is orbital floor blowout fracture, which accounts for roughly 70% of cases and follows blunt periocular trauma. Smaller orbital volumes also fail in silent sinus syndrome, after orbital decompression for thyroid eye disease, with hemifacial atrophy, and from rare metastatic scirrhous breast carcinoma. A Hertel exophthalmometer reading with at least a 2 mm difference between the two eyes is clinically abnormal. Untreated, enophthalmos produces persistent diplopia, facial asymmetry, and a pseudoptosis appearance that affects function and identity. Orbital floor reconstruction with titanium mesh, porous polyethylene, or PDS implants restores volume and globe position in most surgical cases.

keyStats.prevalence: Post-traumatic enophthalmos develops in 20-30% of untreated orbital floor fractures; orbital floor fracture incidence is roughly 18-25 per 100,000 per year in the US (Hopper 2006)
keyStats.demographics: Trauma-driven cases skew male 3-4x and ages 15-40; silent sinus syndrome is more common in adults aged 30-50 with no sex predilection
keyStats.avgAge: Trauma-related onset typically age 15-40; silent sinus syndrome typically 30-50; metastatic causes typically over 50
keyStats.globalCases: No global registry; orbital trauma alone accounts for an estimated 1-2 million new cases of orbital floor fracture annually worldwide

definition: Enophthalmos (ICD-10: H05.4) is a structural disorder of the orbit in which the globe sits posterior to its normal anteroposterior position relative to the bony orbital rim. The pathophysiology is almost always a mismatch between orbital volume and orbital contents: either the bony orbit enlarges (blowout fracture, post-decompression, congenital orbital asymmetry) or the soft tissues contract (fat atrophy, fibrosis, sinus collapse pulling the floor downward). Each 1 mL increase in orbital volume produces approximately 0.8-1.0 mm of posterior globe displacement. The condition is the mechanical opposite of exophthalmos (proptosis), where the globe is pushed forward. A difference of 2 mm or more between the two eyes on Hertel exophthalmometry is the accepted threshold for clinical abnormality, and differences of 3 mm or more are usually cosmetically and functionally noticeable. Enophthalmos is managed by oculoplastic surgeons within ophthalmology, often in collaboration with maxillofacial surgery, otolaryngology (for silent sinus syndrome), and oncology (for metastatic cases). Specific ICD-10 subcodes include H05.40 (unspecified), H05.411-H05.413 (lateralized), and H05.42 (deformity due to trauma or surgery of orbit).

typesClassification.description: The most common form, following orbital floor or medial wall blowout fracture. Onset is delayed by 2-6 weeks as initial swelling resolves and fat herniates into the maxillary sinus, expanding effective orbital volume.

Spontaneous collapse of the maxillary sinus due to chronic ostial obstruction pulls the orbital floor inferiorly, expanding orbital volume. Presents with painless progressive enophthalmos and hypoglobus over months.

Develops after orbital decompression performed for thyroid eye disease or other space-occupying orbital lesions. Over-decompression produces a paradoxical sunken eye and is a recognized complication of three-wall decompression.
Soft-tissue contraction without bony change. Seen in Parry-Romberg syndrome (progressive hemifacial atrophy), linear scleroderma 'en coup de sabre', and post-radiotherapy orbital fat atrophy.

Distinctive paradoxical presentation in which orbital metastasis, most often from scirrhous breast carcinoma, induces dense fibrosis that pulls the globe posteriorly. The eye is sunken rather than proptotic despite the presence of an orbital tumor.

primarySymptoms: Visibly sunken eyeball with a Hertel exophthalmometer difference of 2 mm or more between the affected and unaffected eye, often appreciated by patients or family members in photographs taken before and after the inciting event.
Deep-set superior sulcus with a hollowed appearance above the upper eyelid, often described by patients as a tired or aged look on one side of the face.
Pseudoptosis appearance in which the upper eyelid sits lower on the affected side because the globe has retracted posteriorly, even though the levator palpebrae muscle itself is functioning normally.
Vertical or oblique binocular diplopia, especially on upgaze, caused by entrapment or tethering of the inferior rectus and inferior oblique muscles in an associated orbital floor fracture.
Facial asymmetry with malar flattening on the affected side, most striking in post-traumatic and silent sinus syndrome cases, frequently noticed in mirror photographs and identification documents.
Hypoglobus, a vertical drop of the affected globe relative to the contralateral eye, that often accompanies enophthalmos in floor fractures and silent sinus syndrome.
Limited ocular motility on upgaze and downgaze when extraocular muscles are entrapped within the fracture, producing a positive forced duction test on examination.
Numbness over the cheek, upper lip, and gum (infraorbital nerve distribution) in patients with orbital floor fracture, due to nerve injury within the infraorbital canal.
Painless gradual onset in silent sinus syndrome over weeks to months, with no antecedent trauma reported and minimal subjective discomfort.
Eyelid retraction or apparent lower lid laxity caused by altered globe position rather than primary lid pathology.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent vertical diplopia or upgaze restriction more than two weeks after periocular trauma, even if the eye initially appeared normal
Asymmetric facial photographs in the months following a midface injury or maxillofacial surgery
Unilateral chronic nasal obstruction or maxillary sinus pressure preceding subtle eye position change in silent sinus syndrome
Numbness of the cheek and upper teeth after periocular trauma, signaling infraorbital nerve involvement and likely floor fracture
emergencySigns: Sudden severe eye pain, nausea, and vomiting following orbital trauma in a child or young adult, suggesting a trapdoor fracture with muscle entrapment requiring urgent surgical release within 24-48 hours to prevent ischemic muscle necrosis
Acute loss of vision, afferent pupillary defect, or proptosis followed by sudden retrodisplacement after orbital trauma, raising concern for retrobulbar hemorrhage or globe rupture
Rapidly progressive enophthalmos with proptosis on the contralateral side and weight loss, suggesting metastatic scirrhous carcinoma warranting same-week imaging and oncology referral
New facial swelling, fever, and discharge after orbital reconstruction, suggesting implant infection requiring urgent surgical evaluation

causes.description: The dominant cause, responsible for approximately 70% of enophthalmos cases. Blunt force to the globe transmits hydraulic and buckling pressure to the thin orbital floor, which fractures into the maxillary sinus. Orbital fat and inferior rectus muscle herniate inferiorly, enlarging effective orbital volume and dropping the globe posteriorly and inferiorly.

The lamina papyracea is the thinnest orbital wall and fractures into the ethmoid sinuses. Medial wall fractures contribute to enophthalmos in combination with floor fractures and as isolated injuries, particularly after fist injuries directed at the medial canthus.

Chronic obstruction of the maxillary sinus ostium creates negative intrasinus pressure that gradually pulls the orbital floor inferiorly and collapses the maxillary sinus walls. The condition was first systematically described by Soparkar and colleagues in 1994 and produces painless, progressive enophthalmos and hypoglobus over months.

Surgical removal of one or more orbital walls for severe thyroid eye disease or other orbital decompression indications can over-correct, producing iatrogenic enophthalmos. Three-wall decompression carries the highest risk, and modern technique aims to titrate bone removal to target globe position.

causes.cause: Orbital varices and orbital volume loss
causes.description: Distensible venous malformations within the orbit can produce intermittent enophthalmos that worsens with Valsalva or head-down positioning. Spontaneous thrombosis or surgical resection of varices can also leave residual volume loss.

Progressive hemifacial atrophy is a rare neurocutaneous disorder that produces unilateral atrophy of skin, subcutaneous tissue, muscle, and bone of the face. Orbital fat atrophy and bone remodeling lead to enophthalmos that progresses over years before stabilizing.

A distinctive paradox in oncologic ophthalmology: orbital metastasis from scirrhous breast carcinoma induces dense desmoplastic fibrosis that contracts and pulls the globe posteriorly rather than displacing it forward. Enophthalmos in a postmenopausal woman with a breast cancer history warrants immediate orbital imaging.

Severe ocular trauma can cause the globe itself to shrink (phthisis bulbi) or collapse after open-globe injury, producing apparent enophthalmos that reflects globe volume loss rather than orbital volume change.

riskFactors.description: Demographic group accounting for the majority of orbital floor fractures, with motor-vehicle collisions, assaults, and sports injuries as the dominant mechanisms.

riskFactors.factor: Contact and combat sports without eye protection
riskFactors.description: Basketball, baseball, hockey, boxing, mixed martial arts, and racquet sports all produce orbital fractures at rates that fall sharply with use of polycarbonate eye protection.

Unrestrained occupants and unhelmeted cyclists sustain midface fractures at multiple times the rate of restrained or helmeted counterparts. Airbag deployment changes the pattern but does not eliminate orbital injury.

riskFactors.factor: Chronic maxillary sinusitis and sinus ostial obstruction
riskFactors.description: Long-standing ostial obstruction is the prerequisite for silent sinus syndrome. Patients with allergic rhinitis, nasal polyposis, or septal deviation have a higher background incidence.

riskFactors.factor: History of thyroid eye disease requiring decompression
riskFactors.description: Orbital decompression for compressive optic neuropathy or severe proptosis carries a measurable risk of post-surgical enophthalmos, particularly with aggressive three-wall techniques. Modern balanced two-wall decompression reduces but does not eliminate the risk.

riskFactors.factor: History of breast cancer, particularly lobular or scirrhous subtypes
riskFactors.description: Scirrhous breast carcinoma has a recognized predilection for orbital metastasis with desmoplastic enophthalmos. Any new enophthalmos in a patient with breast cancer warrants orbital MRI.
Linear scleroderma 'en coup de sabre' and Parry-Romberg syndrome are autoimmune-related disorders that can progressively reduce orbital soft-tissue volume.

Radiotherapy for retinoblastoma, orbital lymphoma, or paranasal sinus cancer can cause delayed orbital fat atrophy and bone hypoplasia producing late enophthalmos, particularly when delivered in childhood.
Thinner orbital bones in older adults fracture at lower energy thresholds, increasing the likelihood of orbital floor injury from ground-level falls.

affectedDemographics: Orbital floor fractures affect roughly 18-25 per 100,000 people per year in high-income countries; men outnumber women approximately 3-4 to 1
Pediatric orbital fractures account for 10-15% of cases and have a higher rate of trapdoor (greenstick) fractures requiring urgent surgical release
Silent sinus syndrome is described in adults aged 30-50 with roughly equal sex distribution; under-recognized in primary care settings
Metastatic enophthalmos from breast carcinoma occurs predominantly in postmenopausal women with known or occult lobular or scirrhous primary disease
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of enophthalmos combines a focused history, careful exophthalmometry, and dedicated orbital imaging. The clinician first measures both eyes with a Hertel exophthalmometer braced against the lateral orbital rims; a difference of 2 mm or more between sides is abnormal, and 3 mm or more is almost always cosmetically apparent. The same instrument is used at every follow-up because absolute readings vary between examiners. Worth's four-dot test, Maddox rod, and Hess chart quantify any associated diplopia and document its character (horizontal, vertical, oblique, comitant vs incomitant). Forced duction testing under topical anesthesia distinguishes mechanical restriction from neurogenic motility loss. Computed tomography of the orbits with thin coronal and sagittal reconstructions is the gold standard, demonstrating bony defects, herniated soft tissue, and orbital volume changes; CT also defines the size of any orbital floor defect, which guides surgical decision-making (defects larger than 2 cm² typically warrant repair). Magnetic resonance imaging is reserved for evaluating soft-tissue causes such as orbital varix, metastatic carcinoma, or Parry-Romberg syndrome, where the bony orbit is intact but soft-tissue volume is abnormal. Schirmer testing is added when dry eye symptoms accompany the enophthalmos. Visual acuity, color vision, pupillary response, and intraocular pressure are recorded at every visit because optic nerve compromise can complicate both the original trauma and surgical repair.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the anteroposterior position of each globe relative to the lateral orbital rim. A difference of 2 mm or more between eyes is the clinical threshold for enophthalmos. Serial readings document progression and surgical correction.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician rests a small instrument against both lateral orbital rims and asks the patient to fix on a distant target. Readings are taken from each eye in turn. The test is painless, takes under two minutes, and the base bar setting is recorded so future readings remain comparable.

diagnosticTests.test: CT orbits with coronal and sagittal reconstructions
diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard for diagnosing orbital floor and medial wall fractures, measuring defect size, identifying herniated fat or inferior rectus muscle, and calculating orbital volume. Defines surgical anatomy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A non-contrast CT of the orbits and paranasal sinuses takes 5-10 minutes. The patient lies still with the head supported in a holder. Radiation dose to the lens is minimized by modern multi-detector scanners. Results are usually available the same day.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Preferred for soft-tissue causes including orbital varix, metastatic infiltration, Parry-Romberg syndrome, and post-surgical fat atrophy. Distinguishes fibrotic tumor from inflammatory orbital disease.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An MRI scan lasts 30-45 minutes. The patient lies still in the scanner; a contrast agent (gadolinium) is injected through an arm vein for tumor evaluation. The scan is noisy but painless; sedation is rarely needed in adults.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes mechanical restriction from cranial nerve palsy. Performed in the office under topical anesthetic; the examiner grasps the eye with forceps and attempts passive rotation. A blocked rotation indicates entrapment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A drop of anesthetic is placed on the eye, then forceps gently hold the conjunctiva near the muscle insertion and rotate the eye in each direction. The procedure takes a few minutes and produces brief mild discomfort.

diagnosticTests.test: Hess chart or Lees screen orthoptic assessment
diagnosticTests.purpose: Maps the position of each eye in all gaze directions and quantifies extraocular muscle dysfunction over time. Useful for monitoring spontaneous recovery and planning strabismus correction after trauma.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An orthoptist guides the patient through a series of pointing exercises in front of a special screen. The test takes 10-15 minutes per eye and is repeated at follow-up visits to track change.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies coexisting dry eye, which is common after orbital trauma and reconstruction and can drive ongoing ocular surface symptoms independent of the enophthalmos itself.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small paper strip is placed on the lower eyelid for five minutes. The patient closes the eyes gently. The clinician then measures how far tears have wetted the strip.

diagnosticTests.test: Optical coherence tomography (OCT) and visual fields
diagnosticTests.purpose: Baseline retinal nerve fiber layer and visual field testing before surgical repair, particularly when there is concern for traumatic optic neuropathy or implant-related optic nerve compromise.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: OCT is a quick, painless light-based scan of the retina lasting under five minutes per eye. Visual field testing takes 10-15 minutes per eye and requires the patient to press a button each time a light appears.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of enophthalmos depends on cause, severity, time course, and the patient's functional and cosmetic priorities. Acute trauma cases without diplopia and with less than 2 mm of enophthalmos are often observed for 7-14 days while orbital swelling resolves, then re-imaged before deciding on surgery. Indications for orbital floor repair generally include a defect larger than 2 cm² on CT, persistent symptomatic diplopia, clinical or anticipated enophthalmos of 2 mm or more, and any radiographic muscle entrapment. Pediatric trapdoor fractures with muscle entrapment and the oculocardiac reflex demand urgent operation within 24-48 hours because the entrapped muscle is at risk of ischemic necrosis. The standard approach is transconjunctival or subciliary access to the orbital floor with reconstruction using titanium mesh, porous polyethylene, or polydioxanone (PDS) sheet implants; choice depends on defect size, patient age, and surgeon preference. Implants are precisely contoured to restore native floor anatomy, supporting prolapsed fat and re-establishing globe position. For silent sinus syndrome, the operation is staged or combined: endoscopic maxillary antrostomy by an otolaryngologist relieves the negative-pressure cycle, and the orbital floor is reconstructed at the same setting or in delayed fashion if remodeling occurs spontaneously. Post-decompression enophthalmos is managed with orbital fat grafts, alloplastic implants, or formal floor reconstruction if the defect is large. Underlying disease must be treated when the cause is not structural: hormonal and immunosuppressive control of thyroid eye disease before considering rehabilitative surgery; oncologic management of metastatic breast carcinoma; immunosuppression for active scleroderma. Late presentation (more than six months from trauma) is technically more demanding because scar tissue must be released, and outcomes for enophthalmos correction are best within the first three to four months. Custom 3D-printed patient-specific implants improve precision in complex or revision cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Observation with serial Hertel readings and imaging
medicalTreatments.description: Initial management for traumatic cases without diplopia or significant enophthalmos. Reassessment at 7-14 days after injury when periorbital swelling has resolved clarifies the true defect and globe position. Many small floor fractures self-stabilize without surgery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Approximately 50-60% of small orbital floor fractures without entrapment do not progress to clinically significant enophthalmos and avoid surgery.

medicalTreatments.name: Short-course systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 1 mg/kg taper over 7-10 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Used selectively in the acute post-traumatic window to reduce orbital edema and clarify the surgical decision. Avoided in pediatric trapdoor fractures (urgent operation is the answer) and in patients with infection or significant globe injury.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces edema-related diplopia in roughly 60-70% of cases within 5 days; does not change the underlying need for surgical reconstruction.

medicalTreatments.name: Nasal decongestants and sinus precautions
medicalTreatments.description: After orbital floor fracture, patients are advised to avoid nose blowing, Valsalva, and air travel for 2-4 weeks. Topical oxymetazoline and saline irrigation manage congestion while reducing the risk of orbital emphysema.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prevents complications rather than correcting enophthalmos; orbital emphysema and secondary infection are uncommon when these measures are followed.

medicalTreatments.name: Botulinum toxin to a paretic or contralateral antagonist muscle
medicalTreatments.description: Temporary chemical denervation of an overacting yoke muscle can be used to manage diplopia while waiting for surgical reconstruction or in patients who decline surgery. Effects last approximately 3 months.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves single-image fusion in primary gaze in 50-65% of selected patients during the active period.

medicalTreatments.name: Antibiotics for sinus precaution after floor reconstruction
medicalTreatments.description: Short prophylactic course (typically a 7-day oral cephalosporin or amoxicillin-clavulanate) after orbital floor surgery to reduce the risk of implant infection from contiguous sinus flora.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Implant infection rates remain below 2% with combined prophylaxis, careful technique, and sinus precaution counseling.

medicalTreatments.name: Dry-eye management with preservative-free lubricants and punctal occlusion
medicalTreatments.description: Many patients with enophthalmos, especially after decompression or reconstruction, experience exposure-related dry eye. Frequent lubrication, nocturnal ointment, and punctal plugs maintain ocular surface health.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom relief in 70-85% of patients with appropriate dosing; reduces risk of exposure keratopathy.

surgicalOptions.name: Orbital floor reconstruction with titanium mesh
surgicalOptions.description: Pre-contoured or intraoperatively shaped titanium mesh supports the prolapsed orbital contents and restores floor anatomy. Preferred for large defects, comminuted fractures, and adult patients in whom long-term rigidity is desired. Compatible with future MRI.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Restoration of globe position within 2 mm of the contralateral side in roughly 85-90% of cases when performed within the first 3 months of injury.

surgicalOptions.name: Orbital floor reconstruction with porous polyethylene (Medpor)
surgicalOptions.description: Porous polymer implant that allows fibrovascular ingrowth and integrates with the surrounding tissue. Used for medium-sized defects and in pediatric cases where titanium edges are avoided. Can be combined with embedded titanium support for larger defects.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Successful globe repositioning in 80-90% of selected cases; implant extrusion or infection in under 3%.

surgicalOptions.name: Resorbable PDS (polydioxanone) sheet reconstruction
surgicalOptions.description: Bioresorbable sheet that supports the floor during healing and then dissolves over 6 months. Favored in pediatric patients to preserve growth potential and in selected small to moderate fractures where permanent implants are unnecessary.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Equivalent functional outcomes to non-resorbable implants in defects under 2 cm²; not suitable for large defects due to risk of late prolapse.

surgicalOptions.name: Patient-specific 3D-printed implants
surgicalOptions.description: CT-based virtual planning produces a titanium or polyethylene implant matched to the patient's normal contralateral orbit. Used in complex, comminuted, or revision cases where standard implants give imprecise results.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Sub-millimeter accuracy of globe repositioning in published series; symmetric outcomes in over 90% of complex reconstructions.

surgicalOptions.name: Endoscopic maxillary antrostomy for silent sinus syndrome
surgicalOptions.description: Otolaryngology-performed endoscopic ostial enlargement breaks the negative-pressure cycle. Combined or staged with orbital floor reconstruction by an oculoplastic surgeon to restore globe position. Coordination between specialties improves outcomes.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Resolution of sinus collapse in 95%; partial spontaneous improvement of enophthalmos in 30-40%, with the remainder benefiting from delayed floor reconstruction.

surgicalOptions.name: Orbital fat grafting and dermis-fat graft for atrophic enophthalmos
surgicalOptions.description: Autologous fat or dermis-fat is harvested (most commonly from the lower abdomen) and grafted into the deep orbit to restore volume in post-decompression, post-radiation, and atrophic cases. Partial graft resorption is expected.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Improvement of 1-2 mm in globe projection in 70-80% of cases; durability over 5 years in 60-70% of grafted volume.

alternativeTreatments.name: Custom ocular prosthetics and scleral shells
alternativeTreatments.description: Hand-painted scleral shells crafted by an ocularist can mask mild enophthalmos and improve cosmetic symmetry without surgery, particularly in patients with phthisis bulbi or poor surgical candidates. Used as adjunct rather than substitute when surgical correction is feasible.

alternativeTreatments.name: Photographic and prosthetic camouflage with eyelid surgery alone
alternativeTreatments.description: Selected patients with mild residual enophthalmos benefit from contralateral upper-lid surgery or filler placement in the superior sulcus to balance the appearance of the two eyes. Reserved for fine-tuning after orbital reconstruction.

whySeeSpecialist: An oculoplastic surgeon should be involved whenever enophthalmos exceeds 2 mm on Hertel measurement, when there is associated diplopia, when an orbital floor or medial wall fracture is identified on imaging, when silent sinus syndrome is suspected, or when prior decompression surgery has produced a sunken eye. Pediatric trapdoor fractures with muscle entrapment require urgent (within 24-48 hours) surgical referral to prevent ischemic muscle necrosis. Suspected metastatic enophthalmos in a patient with a breast cancer history demands same-week orbital imaging and oncology coordination.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for board certification in ophthalmology with fellowship training in oculofacial plastic and reconstructive surgery (ASOPRS in the US, BOPSS or equivalent internationally). Ask the surgeon how many orbital floor reconstructions they perform annually, whether they use intraoperative navigation or patient-specific implants for complex cases, and how they coordinate with otolaryngology for silent sinus syndrome cases. Continuity of post-operative orthoptic care and access to high-resolution CT imaging matter as much as surgical volume.
hospitalCriteria: High-resolution multi-detector CT scanner with dedicated orbital protocols available 24/7
Oculoplastic surgery program with fellowship-trained surgeons performing at least 30 orbital reconstructions per year
Multidisciplinary midface trauma capability including otolaryngology, oral and maxillofacial surgery, and neurosurgery
Intraoperative CT or navigation availability for complex and revision cases
keyFacilities: Academic medical centers with combined oculoplastic and craniofacial trauma services
Tertiary eye hospitals with orbital reconstruction programs
Regional trauma centers integrating ophthalmology with maxillofacial surgery
preventionStrategies: Wear ASTM F803-rated polycarbonate eye protection during basketball, baseball, hockey, racquet sports, and combat sports, which reduces ocular injury rates by over 90%
Use motor-vehicle restraints and bicycle helmets with face protection in high-speed contexts to reduce midface fracture severity
Treat chronic maxillary sinusitis and ostial obstruction promptly to lower the risk of silent sinus syndrome over time
Discuss orbital floor monitoring with the surgical team after orbital decompression for thyroid eye disease, particularly when three-wall procedures were performed
Schedule prompt orbital imaging in any patient with a breast cancer history who develops new asymmetric eye position
lifestyleModifications: Avoid nose blowing, sneezing with closed mouth, scuba diving, and high-altitude flight for 2-4 weeks after orbital floor fracture or repair to prevent orbital emphysema
Sleep with the head of bed elevated 30 degrees during the first week after orbital surgery to reduce swelling
Apply cool compresses to the periocular area for the first 48 hours after trauma or surgery, then warm compresses thereafter to encourage resolution of bruising
Use lubricating drops during the day and ointment at night while orbital swelling resolves to protect the ocular surface
Resume aerobic exercise after surgical clearance (typically 2-3 weeks); avoid contact sports for at least 6 weeks following floor reconstruction
Photograph the face from front and three-quarter angles every 3 months in patients with progressive disorders such as Parry-Romberg to document objective change

recommended: Adequate protein intake (1.0-1.2 g/kg per day) during the immediate post-surgical period to support wound healing
Foods rich in vitamin C and zinc (citrus, leafy greens, nuts, seeds, lean meat) to support collagen synthesis
Soft foods for 24-48 hours after orbital reconstruction if facial swelling makes chewing uncomfortable
Adequate hydration with 2 liters of water daily, which supports tissue healing and reduces post-operative constipation from analgesics
avoid: Excessive sodium intake, which prolongs periorbital edema during recovery
Alcohol for at least one week after surgery, which interferes with healing and interacts with analgesics
Hard, crunchy, or hot foods immediately after surgery that could disturb the surgical site or worsen swelling
Concentrated sugar loads that may impair early wound healing in patients with diabetes

exerciseGuidelines: Avoid all contact sports, weight training, and Valsalva-inducing exercise for 4-6 weeks after orbital floor reconstruction. Light walking is encouraged from day one. Aerobic exercise without facial impact (stationary cycling, elliptical) can usually resume by week 3. Return to swimming after 3-4 weeks if the wound is fully healed and the surgeon has cleared the patient. Use polycarbonate eye protection when returning to sport.
dailyManagement: Apply prescribed lubricating drops and ointment on the schedule given by the surgical team and report any new eye redness or discharge
Measure facial symmetry monthly using a fixed-camera selfie taken in the same lighting and posture to track stability
Wear sunglasses outdoors to reduce glare sensitivity that is common after orbital surgery and to protect the healing tissue from UV exposure
Maintain a written log of diplopia changes, headaches, and any numbness in the cheek or upper teeth to bring to follow-up visits
Coordinate with sinus or dental procedures by informing every clinician about the presence of an orbital floor implant
prognosis: With timely surgical repair, the long-term prognosis for traumatic enophthalmos is good. Approximately 85-90% of patients achieve globe position within 2 mm of the contralateral eye when surgery is performed within the first three months of injury, and 70-80% retain stable correction at five years. Diplopia in primary gaze resolves in roughly 80% of patients after combined floor reconstruction and orthoptic rehabilitation; persistent oblique-gaze diplopia is the most common residual symptom and may require staged strabismus surgery. Silent sinus syndrome generally responds well to combined endoscopic and orbital surgery, with stable globe position in 80-90% at three years. Outcomes are less favorable in late repair beyond six months, in revision cases, and when the inciting injury caused traumatic optic neuropathy. Metastatic and progressive autoimmune causes carry the prognosis of the underlying disease, with enophthalmos correction reserved for stable, treated patients. Across all groups, early recognition of muscle entrapment, careful imaging, and appropriately timed surgery are the strongest predictors of a good outcome.
recoveryTimeline: Periorbital swelling peaks at 48-72 hours after surgery and largely resolves over 2-3 weeks. Bruising fades over 2-3 weeks. Diplopia in upgaze often persists for 4-6 weeks while inflammation around the inferior rectus settles. Final globe position is reliably assessed at 3 months when soft-tissue remodeling is complete. Numbness in the cheek and upper teeth can take 6-12 months to recover fully, and some patients retain a small permanent area of altered sensation. Return to work for desk-based occupations is typically possible within 1-2 weeks; return to manual or hazardous work within 4-6 weeks; return to contact sport at 6-8 weeks after surgeon clearance.
complications: Persistent diplopia in oblique or downgaze due to scar tethering of the inferior rectus or inferior oblique muscle, sometimes requiring strabismus surgery 6-12 months after reconstruction
Implant malposition, migration, or extrusion presenting as recurrent enophthalmos, lid retraction, or visible implant edge; revision surgery is required in 3-5% of cases
Infection of the implant or surrounding orbital tissue, which can present as fever, pain, swelling, and discharge and may require implant removal and intravenous antibiotics
Infraorbital nerve injury producing prolonged or permanent cheek and upper-lip numbness, present in up to 30% of orbital floor fractures and a smaller proportion of reconstructions
Traumatic optic neuropathy or surgical optic nerve injury leading to vision loss, rare but devastating, with risk highest in posterior floor and apex surgery
Lower lid malposition (ectropion or retraction) after transconjunctival or subciliary access, more common with subciliary incisions and managed with eyelid surgery if persistent

supportResources.name: American Academy of Ophthalmology — Patient Information on Orbital Fractures
supportResources.description: Authoritative US patient education from the body that publishes the BCSC oculofacial textbook used by training surgeons.

supportResources.name: American Society of Ophthalmic Plastic and Reconstructive Surgery (ASOPRS)
supportResources.description: Find a fellowship-trained oculoplastic surgeon and read patient-facing resources on orbital trauma and reconstruction.

supportResources.name: British Oculoplastic Surgery Society (BOPSS) Patient Information
supportResources.description: UK patient resources on orbital fracture, decompression, and reconstructive eyelid and orbital surgery.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hyperthyroidism with Thyroid Eye Disease (Graves' Orbitopathy)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Thyroid eye disease produces exophthalmos (forward globe displacement) — the mechanical opposite of enophthalmos — through orbital fat expansion and extraocular muscle enlargement. Patients with severe Graves' disease may also develop iatrogenic enophthalmos after over-aggressive orbital decompression, but the natural disease pushes the eye forward, not backward.

confusedWithConditions.name: Bell's Palsy with Facial Asymmetry
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Bell's palsy produces facial asymmetry through unilateral facial nerve weakness with lagophthalmos and brow drop, but the globe sits in its normal anteroposterior position. Hertel measurements are symmetric, and orbital imaging is normal. The asymmetry is dynamic and worsens with facial movement, unlike the fixed sunken-eye appearance of enophthalmos.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cataract with Pseudoptosis Appearance
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cataract does not move the globe; it reduces visual acuity and creates a cloudy pupil. The pseudoptosis of cataract is functional brow lowering with squinting, whereas the pseudoptosis of enophthalmos is the upper lid resting on a posteriorly displaced globe. Hertel measurements distinguish the two within seconds.

confusedWithConditions.name: Glaucoma with Asymmetric Cupping
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Glaucoma is a disease of the optic nerve and produces visual field loss and elevated intraocular pressure, not altered globe position. Patients sometimes attribute a sunken-eye appearance to glaucoma; orbital imaging and Hertel measurement quickly clarify whether the issue is optic neuropathy or true enophthalmos.

relatedConditions.name: Hyperthyroidism
relatedConditions.relevance: Thyroid eye disease drives most orbital decompression surgery, and over-decompression is a recognized cause of iatrogenic enophthalmos.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Sinusitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic maxillary sinus ostial obstruction is the prerequisite for silent sinus syndrome, the second most common cause of enophthalmos.

relatedConditions.name: Bell's Palsy
relatedConditions.relevance: Both conditions produce facial asymmetry and frequently appear in the differential diagnosis of unilateral periocular changes.

relatedConditions.name: Glaucoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Patients undergoing orbital reconstruction should have intraocular pressure monitored, and pre-existing glaucoma alters anesthetic and post-operative care.

relatedConditions.name: Cataract
relatedConditions.relevance: Cataract and enophthalmos coexist in older trauma patients; surgical planning often addresses both during the rehabilitation pathway.

relatedConditions.name: Deviated Nasal Septum
relatedConditions.relevance: Septal deviation contributes to chronic sinus ostial obstruction and is part of the workup in silent sinus syndrome.

faqs.question: What exactly is enophthalmos?
faqs.answer: Enophthalmos is posterior displacement of the eyeball within the bony orbit, producing a sunken-eye appearance. A Hertel exophthalmometer reading with a difference of 2 mm or more between the two eyes is the accepted clinical threshold for abnormality.

faqs.question: What is the most common cause of enophthalmos?
faqs.answer: Orbital floor blowout fracture is the most common cause, accounting for about 70% of cases. Blunt periocular trauma fractures the thin orbital floor into the maxillary sinus, and fat and inferior rectus muscle herniate downward, dropping the globe back and down.

faqs.question: How is enophthalmos measured?
faqs.answer: Enophthalmos is measured with a Hertel exophthalmometer, braced against the lateral orbital rims to read corneal position on each side. A side-to-side difference of 2 mm or more is abnormal. The same instrument and base setting are used at every follow-up.

faqs.question: What is silent sinus syndrome?
faqs.answer: Silent sinus syndrome is painless, progressive enophthalmos and hypoglobus from chronic maxillary sinus ostial obstruction. Negative pressure in the trapped sinus pulls the orbital floor downward over months. Treatment is endoscopic antrostomy plus orbital floor reconstruction.

faqs.question: Does enophthalmos always require surgery?
faqs.answer: No. Small post-traumatic cases without diplopia and with under 2 mm of enophthalmos are often observed for 7-14 days. Surgery is indicated for floor defects larger than 2 cm², persistent diplopia, enophthalmos of 2 mm or more, or any radiographic muscle entrapment.

faqs.question: How soon should orbital floor surgery happen after trauma?
faqs.answer: Pediatric trapdoor fractures with muscle entrapment require operation within 24-48 hours to prevent ischemic muscle necrosis. Most adult fractures are repaired within 2 weeks once swelling resolves, and overall outcomes are best when surgery occurs within the first 3 months.

faqs.question: What materials are used for orbital floor reconstruction?
faqs.answer: The three most common implants are titanium mesh, porous polyethylene (Medpor), and resorbable polydioxanone (PDS) sheet. Patient-specific 3D-printed titanium or polyethylene implants are increasingly used for complex and revision reconstructions.

faqs.question: Can enophthalmos cause double vision?
faqs.answer: Yes. Vertical or oblique binocular diplopia is common when an orbital floor fracture entraps or tethers the inferior rectus or inferior oblique muscle. Diplopia usually improves after surgical repair, though residual oblique-gaze double vision may need strabismus surgery later.

faqs.question: What is the difference between enophthalmos and exophthalmos?
faqs.answer: Enophthalmos is posterior displacement of the eye (sunken in); exophthalmos is forward displacement (bulging out). They are mechanical opposites. Graves' thyroid eye disease is the classic cause of exophthalmos; orbital floor fracture is the classic cause of enophthalmos.

faqs.question: Is enophthalmos dangerous to vision?
faqs.answer: Enophthalmos itself rarely threatens vision. The underlying causes can: trapdoor fractures with muscle entrapment can permanently damage the muscle if not released urgently, and severe orbital trauma can produce traumatic optic neuropathy requiring same-day evaluation.

faqs.question: Can children develop enophthalmos?
faqs.answer: Yes. Pediatric orbital fractures make up 10-15% of cases and have a higher rate of trapdoor fractures, where springy bone entraps the inferior rectus. Children may show minimal bruising but have severe pain, nausea, and vomiting needing surgical release within 24-48 hours.

faqs.question: How is silent sinus syndrome treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment is combined or staged endoscopic maxillary antrostomy by an otolaryngologist plus orbital floor reconstruction by an oculoplastic surgeon. The antrostomy breaks the negative-pressure cycle and the floor reconstruction restores globe position.

faqs.question: What does an orbital CT scan show?
faqs.answer: An orbital CT scan shows the bony walls, the position of orbital fat and extraocular muscles, and the size of any fracture defect. Coronal and sagittal reconstructions reveal herniated tissue in the maxillary or ethmoid sinus and guide whether and how to operate.

faqs.question: Can enophthalmos come back after surgery?
faqs.answer: Recurrent enophthalmos is uncommon when surgery is done within 3 months of injury and the implant is correctly positioned. Late recurrence usually points to implant migration, malposition, or progressive soft-tissue atrophy and warrants repeat CT imaging.

faqs.question: Why does my cheek feel numb after orbital fracture?
faqs.answer: The infraorbital nerve runs through the orbital floor and supplies the cheek, upper lip, and upper teeth. Floor fractures and reconstruction commonly stretch or compress this nerve, producing numbness that recovers in 6-12 months in most patients.

faqs.question: Can breast cancer cause enophthalmos?
faqs.answer: Yes. Metastatic scirrhous breast carcinoma induces dense orbital fibrosis that contracts and pulls the globe posteriorly rather than displacing it forward. New enophthalmos in a patient with a breast cancer history warrants prompt orbital MRI and oncology review.

faqs.question: What is Parry-Romberg syndrome?
faqs.answer: Parry-Romberg syndrome is a rare disorder of progressive hemifacial atrophy affecting skin, fat, muscle, and bone of one side of the face. Orbital fat atrophy and bone remodeling produce enophthalmos that progresses over years before stabilizing.

faqs.question: Will I need other procedures after orbital surgery?
faqs.answer: Many patients do not need new corrective lenses after orbital reconstruction. Some require prism glasses for residual diplopia, and patients with persistent oblique-gaze diplopia may benefit from strabismus surgery 6-12 months after the initial repair.

faqs.question: How long does recovery from orbital floor surgery take?
faqs.answer: Most patients return to desk-based work within 1-2 weeks. Bruising settles over 2-3 weeks and final globe position is assessed at 3 months. Cheek numbness can take 6-12 months to recover, and return to contact sport is typically allowed at 6-8 weeks.

faqs.question: Is there a non-surgical way to fix a sunken eye?
faqs.answer: When surgery is not feasible, custom scleral shells made by an ocularist can mask mild enophthalmos and improve symmetry, particularly in phthisis bulbi. Soft-tissue fillers can balance the upper sulcus in selected cases. These options are adjuncts to structural correction.

faqs.question: Can enophthalmos affect my driving or vision work?
faqs.answer: Enophthalmos with diplopia can temporarily affect depth perception and side vision. Patients with persistent diplopia should not drive until cleared by their ophthalmologist, and pilots and commercial drivers should defer return to duty until binocular single vision is confirmed.

symptomKeywords: facial asymmetry one eye looks smaller
metaDescription: Enophthalmos: posterior eye displacement from orbital fracture or silent sinus syndrome. Hertel measurement, CT diagnosis, reconstruction options reviewed.
